nodes.label: Agents
nodes.short_description: Agent runtimes, frameworks, harnesses, and surfaces that define, host, or coordinate individual and multi-agent systems.
nodes.long_description: Everything whose primary concern is the agent itself — how it is built, executed, composed with others, and exposed to users. Subcategories split by architectural role (framework vs harness vs runtime), composition style (single, multi-agent, networks), and surface (CLI, desktop GUI, frontend). Excludes underlying models, tools, and infrastructure.

nodes.label: Agent Clusters
nodes.short_description: Deployments that run many related agents as a managed cluster sharing state, scheduling, or lifecycle.
nodes.long_description: Clustered agent runtimes where agents are provisioned and operated as a group rather than invoked ad-hoc. Typically a platform or service that handles spawning, identity, and persistence for a fleet of agents under one roof. Distinct from Multiagent (which focuses on reasoning patterns) and Networks (cross-org discovery).

nodes.label: Agent Collaboration
nodes.short_description: Environments where multiple agents cooperate on shared tasks via roles, dialogue, or negotiation.
nodes.long_description: Runtimes and frameworks whose core abstraction is collaborative problem-solving between agents — role assignment, turn-taking, debate, joint planning. Typically a library or sandbox rather than a transport protocol. Differs from Agent-to-Agent (which is about the communication channel) and Teams (preassembled crews).

nodes.label: Agent to Agent
nodes.short_description: Protocols, channels, and libraries that let independent agents address and message one another directly.
nodes.long_description: The communication substrate for inter-agent messaging — discovery, addressing, message format, and session semantics between otherwise separate agents. Usually a protocol or SDK rather than a full orchestration framework. Collaboration patterns built on top belong under Agent Collaboration.

nodes.label: Experiments
nodes.short_description: Experimental setups exploring direct agent-to-agent communication and emergent behaviours.
nodes.long_description: Research-style experiments in which two or more agents interact directly, often probing protocols, negotiation, or voice-to-voice exchange. In-scope: demos and prototypes. Out-of-scope: production multi-agent frameworks.

nodes.label: Voices
nodes.short_description: Experimental voice-based protocols for agent-to-agent communication.
nodes.long_description: Research projects where agents negotiate a more efficient voice channel when they detect they're both machines — e.g. switching to non-human audio encodings. Distinct from standard voice interfaces because the counterparty is another agent, not a human.

nodes.label: Autonomous
nodes.short_description: Long-running agents that pursue goals with minimal human supervision once initialised.
nodes.long_description: Agents designed to operate open-endedly toward a goal, re-planning and acting without step-by-step human prompting. Subcategories specialise by vertical (e.g. Trading). Distinct from task-specific or harness-driven agents that expect tight human-in-the-loop interaction.

nodes.label: Autonomous Agent Creation
nodes.short_description: Tools where an agent generates, configures, or spawns new agents autonomously.
nodes.long_description: Meta-level systems in which an agent's output is another agent — skill generation, self-extension, or dynamic agent bootstrapping. The interface is typically a runtime or SDK that exposes agent-creation as a first-class action. Excludes human-driven low-code agent builders.

nodes.label: Trading
nodes.short_description: Autonomous agents that execute financial trading strategies end-to-end.
nodes.long_description: LLM-driven agents that analyse markets and place or simulate trades with minimal human intervention. Typically standalone apps combining data ingestion, strategy reasoning, and broker/exchange APIs. Sits under Autonomous because the agent closes the loop without a human in the loop.

nodes.label: Backend Platforms
nodes.short_description: Hosted or self-hostable backends that provide APIs and infrastructure for running user-defined agents.
nodes.long_description: Server-side platforms (often with a studio UI) that handle agent definition, execution, state, and API exposure as a managed service. Typically LLMOps-adjacent products like Dify. Distinct from Frameworks (libraries) and Frontends (chat UIs).

nodes.label: CLIs/Toolkits
nodes.short_description: Command-line tools and SDKs for defining, running, and debugging agents from a terminal.
nodes.long_description: CLI-first developer toolkits for agent construction and execution — scaffolding, local runs, tool wiring. Vendor-branded CLIs live under the Vendor child. Excludes coding-assistant CLIs (which belong under Development) and full GUI harnesses.

nodes.label: Vendor
nodes.short_description: First-party agent CLIs and toolkits shipped by model vendors.
nodes.long_description: CLI-based agent harnesses released directly by model providers (e.g. Qwen, Anthropic, OpenAI) as the canonical way to drive their models agentically. Distinct from third-party CLIs that wrap multiple vendors' models.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Agents that drive a full computer via screen, keyboard, and mouse to accomplish user tasks.
nodes.long_description: Agents whose action space is general desktop/OS control — screenshots, clicks, keystrokes — rather than narrow APIs or browsers alone. Includes local harnesses that wrap vision-language models to operate a machine. Browser-only automation and narrow RPA belong elsewhere.

nodes.label: Local Computer-Use Harnesses
nodes.short_description: Harnesses that run computer-use agents locally on the user's own machine.
nodes.long_description: Projects that let an LLM drive the local OS — keyboard, mouse, screen, shell — rather than a sandboxed remote VM. Typically Python apps wrapping a VLM and OS automation libraries. Distinct from cloud-hosted computer-use services.

nodes.label: Desktop GUIs
nodes.short_description: Native desktop applications that package agents as installable GUI software.
nodes.long_description: Installable Mac/Windows/Linux apps whose primary surface is a desktop GUI for interacting with one or more agents. Distinct from web Frontends and terminal CLIs. The focus is packaging and UX, not the underlying agent logic.

nodes.label: Development
nodes.short_description: Agents whose purpose is to write, modify, and ship software end-to-end.
nodes.long_description: Coding agents that take engineering tasks — implement features, fix bugs, refactor, open PRs — usually via a harness over a code workspace. Examples like OpenHands sit here. Excludes IDE copilots focused on inline completion and generic frameworks.

nodes.label: Domain Specific
nodes.short_description: Agents tailored to the workflows and data of a particular industry or knowledge domain.
nodes.long_description: Vertical agents whose design is shaped by domain constraints (biotech, legal, finance, etc.) rather than generic task-solving. Subcategories split by domain. Differs from Task Specific, which groups by the kind of task performed rather than the field it operates in.

nodes.label: Biotech
nodes.short_description: Agents tailored to biotech and life-sciences research workflows.
nodes.long_description: Domain-specific agents for biology, genomics, and biomedical research — e.g. orchestrating bioinformatics tools and literature reasoning. Usually libraries or research platforms grounded in domain tool APIs. Sibling to other verticals under Domain Specific.

nodes.label: Frameworks
nodes.short_description: Libraries for defining agents, tools, memory, and control flow in application code.
nodes.long_description: Developer-facing frameworks used to build agents programmatically — agent loops, tool schemas, state, handoffs. Typically imported as a library rather than run as a service. Open-source exemplars live under the Open Source child; distinct from hosted Backend Platforms.

nodes.label: Open Source
nodes.short_description: Open-source agent frameworks usable as libraries for building custom agents.
nodes.long_description: General-purpose frameworks for defining agents, tools, and loops, released under an open-source licence. Distinct from vendor-locked or proprietary frameworks. Typically a Python or TypeScript library.

nodes.label: Frontends
nodes.short_description: User-facing chat and control UIs for interacting with agents built elsewhere.
nodes.long_description: Web or app frontends that sit in front of an agent backend — chat panels, mission-control dashboards, session views. The agent logic and models are assumed to live behind the UI. Distinct from Desktop GUIs (native apps) and full Backend Platforms.

nodes.label: Harnesses
nodes.short_description: Outer control loops that drive a model through tool use, planning, and iteration on a task.
nodes.long_description: Execution harnesses that wrap an LLM with a scaffolding loop — observe, plan, act, reflect — often with tool access and workspace mounts. Typically a runnable binary or CLI rather than a library. Frameworks provide building blocks; harnesses provide the whole loop.

nodes.label: CLIs
nodes.short_description: Command-line harnesses that wrap LLMs into interactive coding or task agents.
nodes.long_description: CLI-first agent harnesses (e.g. Claude Code, Aider-style tools) that run an agent loop in the terminal with shell and file access. Children split by vendor-first-party vs third-party. Distinct from GUI or IDE integrations.

nodes.label: Third Party
nodes.short_description: Third-party CLI agent harnesses not shipped by a model vendor.
nodes.long_description: Terminal-based coding/agent harnesses built by independent teams that target multiple models via provider APIs. Contrast with vendor CLIs (Claude Code, Gemini CLI) that are tied to a specific model provider.

nodes.label: Vendor
nodes.short_description: First-party CLI agent harnesses shipped by model vendors for their own models.
nodes.long_description: Terminal coding agents published by the company behind the underlying model (e.g. Gemini CLI, Claude Code). Distinct from third-party CLIs which are model-agnostic and built by independent developers.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.short_description: Memory subsystems that give agents persistent, recallable state across turns and sessions.
nodes.long_description: Components dedicated to agent memory — episodic logs, semantic stores, knowledge graphs, summarisation layers — exposed to agents as a read/write interface. Graph Builders live as a child. Distinct from general-purpose vector databases, which sit under Context/Data.

nodes.label: Graph Builders
nodes.short_description: Libraries that construct and maintain knowledge graphs as agent long-term memory.
nodes.long_description: Tools that extract entities and relations from agent interactions and persist them as a queryable graph for recall. Typically a Python library plus graph DB backend. Sibling to vector-store and episodic-memory approaches under Memory.

nodes.label: Multiagent
nodes.short_description: Systems whose defining abstraction is many agents reasoning or acting in parallel.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks and experiments centred on multi-agent dynamics — large-scale simulations, swarm prediction, emergent collaboration — rather than the plumbing of pairwise messaging. Children cover large-scale experiments and swarm prediction. Agent-to-Agent protocols and cluster runtimes are separate slots.

nodes.label: Large Scale Experiments
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for running multi-agent experiments at population scale — thousands to millions of agents.
nodes.long_description: Simulation platforms designed to scale agent populations far beyond typical crew sizes, for agent-based modelling and emergent-behaviour studies. Typically GPU-accelerated differentiable simulators. Distinct from small production crews and from Swarm Prediction (which is forecasting-focused).

nodes.label: Swarm Prediction
nodes.short_description: Using collective agent swarms to generate forecasts or predictions.
nodes.long_description: Projects that aggregate the outputs of many agents into a prediction signal, drawing on wisdom-of-crowds dynamics. Distinct from generic large-scale experiments in that the goal is a forecast artefact, not behavioural study.

nodes.label: Networks
nodes.short_description: Open networks where agents from different authors or vendors can be discovered and invoked.
nodes.long_description: Directories, marketplaces, and protocol networks exposing many third-party agents behind a shared discovery and invocation layer. The unit is the network itself, not a single cluster. Distinct from Agent Clusters (single-operator fleets) and Agent-to-Agent (raw transport).

nodes.label: Personification
nodes.short_description: Agents given a persistent persona, voice, or identity as their primary design feature.
nodes.long_description: Projects where the agent is presented as a character — name, backstory, consistent voice — rather than a neutral tool. Typically companion-style or branded assistants. Distinct from role-based collaboration setups where personas are ephemeral scaffolding.

nodes.label: Purpose Specific
nodes.short_description: Agents built around a single well-defined purpose or task type.
nodes.long_description: Narrow agents whose scope is one job (e.g. penetration testing) rather than a general assistant or domain generalist. Children enumerate the specific purposes. Differs from Domain Specific (defined by field of application) and Task Specific (defined by task shape).

nodes.label: Penetration Testing
nodes.short_description: Agents purpose-built to perform offensive security and penetration testing tasks.
nodes.long_description: Autonomous or semi-autonomous agents that plan and execute pentest workflows — recon, exploitation, reporting — wrapping security tooling. Typically delivered as a standalone app or platform. Distinct from general-purpose coding or ops agents.

nodes.label: Simulations & Worlds
nodes.short_description: Agent-populated simulations of worlds, societies, or characters used for research, gameplay, or scenario exploration.
nodes.long_description: Covers frameworks and projects where LLM agents inhabit simulated environments — geopolitical wargames, societal models, generated worlds, and roleplay characters. In-scope: multi-agent simulation runtimes and world-generation tools. Out-of-scope: single-task autonomous agents or general multi-agent orchestration frameworks.

nodes.label: Character & Roleplay
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for character-driven roleplay agents, including persona definition, lore, and runtimes.
nodes.long_description: Covers tools for defining roleplay characters, building lore/worldbuilding, and running persistent roleplay sessions. Distinct from societal or geopolitical simulations in that the focus is narrative persona fidelity rather than aggregate dynamics.

nodes.label: Character Definition
nodes.short_description: Schemas, formats, and tools for defining roleplay characters and personas.
nodes.long_description: Specifications and authoring tools that describe a character's traits, voice, backstory, and behaviour for use in roleplay runtimes. Sits upstream of the runtime that executes the character and distinct from world/lore authoring.

nodes.label: Lore & Worldbuilding
nodes.short_description: Authoring tools for the lore, setting, and worldbuilding that surrounds roleplay characters.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks for defining the shared universe — factions, history, locations, rules — that characters in a roleplay simulation inhabit. Distinct from per-character definition (which describes individual personas) and from runtimes (which execute the scene).

nodes.label: Roleplay Runtimes
nodes.short_description: Runtime engines that execute character roleplay sessions with LLM-driven personas.
nodes.long_description: Servers or libraries that take character definitions plus lore and drive live multi-turn roleplay, managing memory, turn order, and scene state. Distinct from the static authoring layers (character definition, lore) which feed into them.

nodes.label: Geopolitical Simulation
nodes.short_description: Agent simulations of geopolitical actors, wargames, and international scenarios.
nodes.long_description: Platforms that model states, factions, and decision-makers as LLM agents for wargaming or policy exploration. Usually research-grade simulators with scenario scripting. Sibling to Societal Simulations (domestic/social) and World Generation (fictional worldbuilding).

nodes.label: Societal Simulations
nodes.short_description: Multi-agent simulations modelling human societies, populations, and social dynamics at scale.
nodes.long_description: Environments where many LLM or rule-based agents interact as a synthetic society to study emergent social behaviour, opinion dynamics, or collective action. Typically research-oriented frameworks or sandboxes. Distinct from geopolitical simulation (state actors) and character roleplay (narrative personas).

nodes.label: World Generation
nodes.short_description: Procedural or agent-driven generation of simulated 3D/2D worlds and environments.
nodes.long_description: Tools and engines that create the worlds agents inhabit — terrain, scenes, physics, and interactive assets — as opposed to populating them with societies or characters. Typically game engines, ML-powered generators, or MCP bridges into DCC tools.

nodes.label: MCP
nodes.short_description: MCP servers exposing world-generation engines (e.g. Unreal) as tools for agents.
nodes.long_description: Model Context Protocol bridges that let agents drive 3D engines and DCC tools to generate or manipulate worlds programmatically. Specific to MCP as the integration surface, distinct from native SDK or plugin-based generators.

nodes.label: Synthesised Perspectives
nodes.short_description: Orchestrating multiple LLM personas to surface a synthesised range of viewpoints on a question.
nodes.long_description: Tools that poll diverse agent personas (demographic, expert, or ideological) and aggregate their outputs into a composite perspective or deliberation. Typically a Python library or notebook pattern. Distinct from general multi-agent teams in that the point is viewpoint diversity, not task decomposition.

nodes.label: Task Specific
nodes.short_description: Agents built to execute a single, well-defined task rather than serving as general-purpose assistants.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for agents narrowly scoped to one workflow (e.g. product design, spec drafting). Contrasts with Domain Specific (broad vertical) and Purpose Specific (broad intent) by narrowing to a concrete task. Usually shipped as standalone apps or scripts.

nodes.label: Product Design
nodes.short_description: Agents specialised for product and UI/UX design workflows.
nodes.long_description: Domain-specific agents that generate, critique, or iterate on product design artifacts — mockups, design systems, component layouts. Distinct from general coding agents by their focus on visual/design output and design-tool integration.

nodes.label: Team Formation
nodes.short_description: Tools that assemble agent teams by selecting roles, skills, and configurations for a given goal.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and services that dynamically compose multi-agent teams — choosing which agents, what roles, and how they coordinate — rather than running pre-defined squads. Sits upstream of multi-agent runtimes; output is a team definition consumed by a framework like CrewAI or AutoGen.

nodes.label: Teams
nodes.short_description: Pre-configured agent teams targeting a specific workflow such as data science or research.
nodes.long_description: Ready-made squads of specialised agents shipped as a unit for a defined workflow (e.g. data science pipelines). Distinct from Team Formation (which builds teams) and generic Multiagent frameworks (which provide primitives) — these are opinionated, domain-shaped teams.

nodes.label: Tools
nodes.short_description: Tooling and integrations that agents call as capabilities, grouped by function.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for discrete tools/capabilities exposed to agents — e.g. communications, search, actuation — rather than frameworks or runtimes. Children narrow by tool category. Out-of-scope: full agent frameworks or MCP servers (which live elsewhere).

nodes.label: Comms
nodes.short_description: Communication tools agents use to message, email, or otherwise talk to humans and other agents.
nodes.long_description: Tool-layer integrations that expose messaging channels (email, chat, MCP mail) as callable capabilities for agents. Usually MCP servers or SDK adapters. Sits under Agents → Tools alongside other tool categories an agent can invoke.

nodes.label: Vision
nodes.short_description: Agents whose primary input is live visual streams — camera, video, or screen — for perception and action.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks and runtimes for agents that reason over real-time vision (webcam, RTSP, screen capture). Typically built on VLMs plus streaming infrastructure. Distinct from Computer Use (which targets OS control) in that the emphasis is perception pipelines, not GUI actuation.

nodes.label: Builders & Platforms
nodes.short_description: Platforms and products for building, deploying, and operating agents — the IDE and PaaS layer of the ecosystem.
nodes.long_description: Top-level segment covering the opinionated tooling stack sitting above raw frameworks: visual builders, hosted runtimes, deployment targets, and optimisation suites. Distinct from Frameworks (libraries) and Infrastructure (lower-level runtimes) by bundling UX and managed execution.

nodes.label: Automation
nodes.short_description: Automation platforms where agents orchestrate workflows across apps and services.
nodes.long_description: Workflow automation tools (Zapier-like, n8n-like) specifically oriented toward AI agent steps and LLM-driven branching. Distinct from pure deployment or development platforms by their event/trigger-driven, integration-heavy model.

nodes.label: Open Source
nodes.short_description: Open-source automation builders for wiring agents, APIs, and workflows without vendor lock-in.
nodes.long_description: Self-hostable automation platforms where users assemble agent and integration workflows. Distinguished from proprietary SaaS automation by source availability and deployability on own infrastructure. Covers both headless engines and GUI-based flow builders.

nodes.label: GUI Based
nodes.short_description: Open-source automation platforms offering a drag-and-drop graphical flow builder.
nodes.long_description: Self-hostable automation tools with a visual canvas for building flows across apps, APIs, and LLMs. Distinguished from code-first open-source automation by the GUI authoring surface. Typically deployed via Docker with a browser-based editor.

nodes.label: Deployment Platforms
nodes.short_description: Managed platforms for hosting and running agents in production.
nodes.long_description: PaaS-style services that take a built agent and handle runtime concerns — scaling, scheduling, secrets, observability. Distinct from development platforms (where you author) and engineering platforms (which target engineering orgs).

nodes.label: Serverless
nodes.short_description: Serverless runtimes for hosting agents without managing long-lived infrastructure.
nodes.long_description: Managed execution environments that run agent code on demand, billing per invocation or session. Typically offered as a service with SDKs or CLI deploy commands. Sits above raw cloud functions, adding agent-aware primitives like session state and long-running tasks.

nodes.label: Development Platforms
nodes.short_description: All-in-one environments for authoring, testing, and iterating on agents.
nodes.long_description: Studios and IDE-like products where agents are designed, prompted, wired to tools, and tested — typically with visual canvases or integrated debugging. Distinct from deployment platforms (runtime hosting) and SaaS builders (end-user app output).

nodes.label: Engineering Platforms
nodes.short_description: Engineering-team-oriented platforms for building and operating agents as software systems.
nodes.long_description: Developer platforms that treat agents as code — version control, typed interfaces, CI, observability — aimed at software engineering orgs rather than citizen developers. Distinct from no-code SaaS builders and general development studios.

nodes.label: On Device Agents
nodes.short_description: Platforms for running agents directly on end-user or edge devices rather than in the cloud.
nodes.long_description: Runtimes and toolkits targeting SBCs, phones, and local hardware where the agent executes on-device. Distinct from cloud deployment platforms by their focus on local inference, constrained resources, and device-native integrations.

nodes.label: Android
nodes.short_description: Agent runtimes targeting Android handsets and tablets as the execution surface.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks and apps that let agents act on an Android device — reading screens, driving apps, and invoking system APIs. Distinguished from SBC targets by the mobile OS and app-permission model. Usually ship as APKs or on-device services.

nodes.label: SBCs
nodes.short_description: Agent runtimes for single-board computers like Raspberry Pi and similar edge hardware.
nodes.long_description: Projects deploying agents to small Linux SBCs for edge, embedded, or hobbyist use. Distinguished from Android by running full Linux with GPIO/hardware access rather than a mobile app sandbox. Typically distributed as images or install scripts.

nodes.label: Optimisation Platform
nodes.short_description: Platforms for evaluating, tuning, and optimising deployed agents and prompts.
nodes.long_description: Hosted suites that close the loop on agent performance — running evals, A/B tests, prompt/flow optimisation, and cost/quality tuning. Distinct from development platforms (authoring) and pure eval frameworks by their integrated, platform-level scope.

nodes.label: Platforms
nodes.short_description: General-purpose agent platforms that don't fit a narrower builder/deployer/engineering slot.
nodes.long_description: Catch-all for broad platforms spanning multiple stages of the agent lifecycle, including embodied-agent platforms. Use more specific sibling categories when applicable; this slot should stay small.

nodes.label: Embodied
nodes.short_description: Platforms for building agents embodied in physical or avatar form factors.
nodes.long_description: Builder platforms targeting embodied agents — robots, avatars, or physical systems — rather than pure software workflows. Provides primitives for perception, actuation, or persona alongside LLM reasoning. Delivered as SDKs or managed services.

nodes.label: SaaS Builders
nodes.short_description: Platforms where agents build or become end-user SaaS applications.
nodes.long_description: Tools that generate full applications — backends, UIs, integrations — via agents, targeting no-code, visual-programming, or code-defined authoring styles. Distinct from development platforms that output agents themselves rather than agent-powered SaaS.

nodes.label: Code-Defined
nodes.short_description: SaaS agent builders where workflows are authored in code rather than visual canvases.
nodes.long_description: Hosted platforms for building agents by writing code — typically Python or TypeScript — against a managed runtime. Sibling to no-code and visual-programming builders, trading accessibility for flexibility and versionability. Positioned between frameworks and fully managed no-code tools.

nodes.label: No Code
nodes.short_description: No-code SaaS builders that let non-developers assemble agents through forms and prompts.
nodes.long_description: Hosted builders aimed at non-technical users, exposing agents via configuration UIs without requiring code or visual graph editing. Distinguished from visual programming by the absence of a node-and-edge canvas. Typically SaaS with template libraries and managed hosting.

nodes.label: Visual Programming
nodes.short_description: Visual node-based builders where agents and workflows are composed on a canvas.
nodes.long_description: SaaS and open platforms providing a graph editor of nodes and edges to wire LLM calls, tools, and control flow. Distinguished from pure no-code form builders by the explicit visual dataflow model. Often used for both automation and agent prototyping.

nodes.label: Workflow Focused
nodes.short_description: Visual SaaS builders optimised for multi-step workflows rather than conversational agents.
nodes.long_description: Visual-programming platforms whose primary unit is a linear or branching workflow chaining LLM and tool calls. Distinguished from conversational or agentic-loop builders by a workflow-centric execution model. Delivered as hosted SaaS with integration libraries.

nodes.label: Context, Memory & Data
nodes.short_description: Everything that feeds agents with context, persistent memory, and structured data.
nodes.long_description: Top-level domain covering how agents acquire, retain, and retrieve information: context assembly, memory stores, and data pipelines. Sits between raw storage/infra and the agent runtime. Children split the space into Context, Memory, and Data.

nodes.label: Context
nodes.short_description: Tooling for assembling, shaping, and optimising the context window sent to LLMs.
nodes.long_description: Covers engines, optimisers, frontend capture tools, and visualisers that decide what goes into a prompt at inference time. Distinguished from Memory (persistent state) and Data (raw sources) by focus on the ephemeral context payload. Typical interface: libraries, IDE plugins, or middleware.

nodes.label: Context Optimisation
nodes.short_description: Tools that shrink, rank, or restructure context to fit window and cost budgets.
nodes.long_description: Focused on pre-processing context before it hits the model — compression, pruning, reranking, deduplication. Distinguished from Engines (assembly runtime) and Optimisation (in-thread/cross-thread strategies) by targeting the payload itself. Delivered as libraries or middleware.

nodes.label: Engines
nodes.short_description: Context engines that orchestrate retrieval, assembly, and delivery of prompt context.
nodes.long_description: Runtime systems responsible for producing the full context payload at each LLM call, coordinating retrievers, rankers, and formatters. Distinguished from optimisation (a transform) and tools (user-facing utilities) by being the assembly runtime. Usually embedded as a library or service.

nodes.label: Frontend Tools
nodes.short_description: Frontend capture tools that grab UI, DOM, or screen state as context for agents.
nodes.long_description: Browser or editor utilities letting users or agents pull live frontend state — DOM nodes, React trees, screenshots — into a prompt. Distinguished from backend context engines by operating at the UI layer. Typically shipped as browser extensions or client libraries.

nodes.label: Optimisation
nodes.short_description: Strategies for keeping context lean over the life of a conversation or agent loop.
nodes.long_description: Covers techniques like in-thread truncation and cross-thread compression that manage context across many turns. Distinguished from single-call Context Optimisation by focusing on conversation-scale dynamics. Children split by scope: within a thread vs across threads.

nodes.label: Cross-Thread Compression
nodes.short_description: Techniques that compress context across multiple conversation threads or sessions into compact summaries.
nodes.long_description: Covers tools that summarise or distil history spanning many threads so agents carry forward global context efficiently. Distinct from In-Thread Truncation, which operates within a single conversation; this slot works across session boundaries.

nodes.label: In-Thread Truncation
nodes.short_description: Within-conversation strategies that prune, summarise, or window context to fit model limits.
nodes.long_description: Covers truncation, sliding windows, and mid-thread summarisation that keep a single conversation under token budgets. Distinct from Cross-Thread Compression by operating only on the live thread's messages.

nodes.label: Tools
nodes.short_description: General utilities for inspecting, editing, and managing context payloads.
nodes.long_description: Catch-all for user-facing context helpers — context managers, editors, inspectors — that don't fit engines or optimisers. Distinguished from engines (runtime) by being interactive tools. Typically CLIs, desktop apps, or IDE integrations.

nodes.label: Context Managers
nodes.short_description: Utilities for assembling, versioning, and injecting prompt context bundles into agents.
nodes.long_description: Covers tools (e.g., dotai) that let developers curate reusable context packs — files, rules, snippets — and load them into agent sessions. Sits above raw prompt templating by managing context as structured, persistent artefacts.

nodes.label: Visualisation
nodes.short_description: Visualisers that render context windows, token budgets, and prompt structure.
nodes.long_description: Tools that let developers see what's inside a context window — tokens, segments, sources, costs. Distinguished from optimisation (which transforms) and engines (which assemble) by being read-only inspection. Usually ship as dashboards, IDE panels, or web apps.

nodes.label: Data
nodes.short_description: Data layer feeding agents — embeddings, RAG, vector stores, search, and grounding.
nodes.long_description: Infrastructure and pipelines that turn raw sources into retrievable, LLM-consumable data. Distinguished from Context (window assembly) and Memory (agent state) by focus on the underlying data plane. Includes embedding pipelines, vector DBs, semantic search, grounding, and storage.

nodes.label: Embedding Pipelines
nodes.short_description: Orchestrators that chunk, embed, and upsert content into vector stores on a schedule or event.
nodes.long_description: Covers pipeline tools that transform source data into embeddings and load them into vector databases, handling chunking, batching, and incremental sync. Distinct from Vector Databases (the store) and RAG Ingestion (end-to-end retrieval pipelines); this slot is the dedicated embedding ETL layer.

nodes.label: Grounding
nodes.short_description: Feeding agents fresh, authoritative external facts at inference time to reduce hallucination.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for data sources, grounding libraries, and search utilities used to anchor LLM output in verifiable references. Distinct from RAG (retrieval pipelines) and Semantic Search (query engines) by focusing on provenance and factuality rather than the retrieval mechanism itself.

nodes.label: Data Sources
nodes.short_description: Curated external datasets and feeds used as authoritative grounding material for agents.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for upstream data providers (financial, news, reference) plugged into agents to ground answers. Distinct from Grounding Tools (the libraries) and Search Utilities (the lookup layer); this is the source side of the grounding stack.

nodes.label: Financial
nodes.short_description: Financial market and company data feeds used to ground agents in quantitative and fundamental data.
nodes.long_description: Data platforms and SDKs exposing equities, fundamentals, macro, and alternative financial data for retrieval by agents. Sits under Grounding/Data Sources as a domain-specific feed, not a generic API connector. Typically consumed as a Python library or REST API.

nodes.label: Grounding Tools
nodes.short_description: Libraries that attach citations, provenance, and verification to LLM outputs.
nodes.long_description: Covers software that wraps LLM calls with source tracking, citation insertion, and factual verification against grounding corpora. Distinct from raw Data Sources and Search Utilities by focusing on the binding between generation and evidence.

nodes.label: Search Utilities
nodes.short_description: Lightweight search APIs and clients used by agents to fetch grounding snippets on demand.
nodes.long_description: Covers web/search APIs and helper libraries agents call to pull fresh evidence at inference time. Distinct from Data Sources (the underlying corpora) and Grounding Tools (citation binding); this slot is the query/fetch convenience layer.

nodes.label: RAG
nodes.short_description: Retrieval-Augmented Generation stacks: engines, ingestion, rerankers, and KG builders that feed LLMs.
nodes.long_description: Parent category for tools implementing RAG pipelines — from document parsing and ingestion to retrieval engines, rerankers, and knowledge-graph construction. Distinct from raw Vector Databases or Semantic Search by covering the full retrieval-to-generation workflow used by agents.

nodes.label: Document Parsing for LLMs
nodes.short_description: Parsers that convert PDFs, Office files, and HTML into LLM-ready structured text and markdown.
nodes.long_description: Covers tools (Docling, Marker, LlamaParse, Unstructured) that extract clean text, tables, and layout from heterogeneous documents for downstream RAG ingestion. Distinct from general Ingestion pipelines; this slot is the parsing primitive feeding them.

nodes.label: Engines
nodes.short_description: End-to-end RAG engines that bundle parsing, indexing, retrieval, and generation into a single runnable service.
nodes.long_description: Integrated retrieval-augmented generation platforms exposing a server or library that handles the full pipeline from document ingest through grounded answer synthesis. Distinct from the standalone ingestion, reranker, and KG-construction siblings, which cover single pipeline stages. Typically deployed as a service with a query API.

nodes.label: Ingestion
nodes.short_description: Pipelines that pull documents from sources and prepare them for embedding and indexing in a RAG stack.
nodes.long_description: Tools focused on the source-to-chunk stage of RAG: connectors, crawlers, chunking, and normalization before vectors are written. Excludes downstream retrieval, reranking, or full engines. Usually libraries or workers plugged into a larger RAG runtime.

nodes.label: Knowledge Graph Construction
nodes.short_description: Building entity-and-relation knowledge graphs from unstructured text to power graph-aware retrieval.
nodes.long_description: Libraries that extract nodes and edges from documents and expose graph-based retrieval strategies as an alternative or complement to pure vector RAG. Sibling to plain ingestion and reranking, which stay in the vector-chunk paradigm. Typically a Python library writing to a graph or hybrid store.

nodes.label: Rerankers
nodes.short_description: Models and services that reorder retrieved candidates by relevance before they reach the LLM.
nodes.long_description: Cross-encoder or late-interaction rerankers consumed as a hosted API or local model, inserted between a first-stage retriever and the generator. Distinct from embedding-based retrieval and full engines; concerned only with precision on a candidate list. Used as a library call or REST endpoint.

nodes.label: Semantic Search
nodes.short_description: Embedding-based search engines and libraries that match queries by meaning rather than keywords.
nodes.long_description: Tools and services for vector/semantic similarity search over code, docs, or arbitrary corpora. Distinct from Vector Databases (the backing store) and RAG (full generation pipelines); this slot is the query-side engine exposing search APIs or CLIs.

nodes.label: Storage
nodes.short_description: General-purpose data stores and backend query layers used by agents outside vector-specific workloads.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for databases and query backends (SQL, NoSQL, object stores) that agents read and write through. Distinct from Vector Databases, which live in their own sibling; this covers the non-vector persistence substrate and its query interfaces.

nodes.label: Backend Querying
nodes.short_description: Querying layers that let agents and LLMs talk to existing databases and backends in natural language.
nodes.long_description: Federated query engines that sit over SQL, NoSQL, and SaaS backends and expose an LLM-facing interface, often as virtual tables or an MCP-style tool. Sibling to raw Databases: this slot is about the query federation layer, not the storage itself.

nodes.label: Databases
nodes.short_description: Databases used as persistent storage for agent data, documents, vectors, and context.
nodes.long_description: Storage engines (vector, relational, graph, or hybrid) that back agent memory and RAG pipelines. Sibling to Backend Querying, which federates access; this slot houses the storage products themselves and specialized children like context databases.

nodes.label: Context Databases
nodes.short_description: Databases purpose-built for storing and querying agent context, sessions, and conversational state.
nodes.long_description: Storage systems optimized for the structure of agent context—turns, tool calls, embeddings, and scoped memories—rather than general-purpose documents or vectors. Sibling-free under Databases; the slot exists to separate context-native stores from commodity vector or relational DBs.

nodes.label: Vector Databases
nodes.short_description: Purpose-built stores for embeddings supporting ANN search, filtering, and hybrid retrieval.
nodes.long_description: Covers standalone vector database products exposing indexing, similarity search, and metadata filtering as a service or library (e.g., Qdrant). Distinct from Semantic Search engines that sit atop them and from general Storage; this is the persistence layer for embeddings.

nodes.label: Memory
nodes.short_description: Persistent memory systems that let agents remember across sessions, users, and time.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and services providing long-term, episodic, or skill memory beyond the context window. Distinguished from Context (per-call assembly) and Data (source corpora) by stateful, agent-scoped retention. Typical interfaces: SDKs, memory-as-a-service APIs, or file-backed stores.

nodes.label: Episodic / Temporal Memory
nodes.short_description: Time-aware memory stores that record events and their temporal relations for agent recall.
nodes.long_description: Covers libraries and graphs that model agent memories as time-stamped episodes or temporal knowledge graphs, enabling 'what happened when' queries. Distinct from Memory-as-a-Service (hosted generic memory) and Session State (per-conversation scratch); this is specifically temporal/episodic recall.

nodes.label: File
nodes.short_description: File-backed memory formats that encode agent state into portable files such as videos or documents.
nodes.long_description: Tools that persist memory as self-contained files (e.g., Memvid encodes embeddings into video). Distinct from hosted Memory-as-a-Service and database-backed episodic memory; the defining trait is a file as the unit of memory storage and exchange.

nodes.label: Memory-as-a-Service
nodes.short_description: Hosted memory backends exposing add/search/forget APIs so agents can outsource long-term recall.
nodes.long_description: Managed services (Zep, Mem0, Letta Cloud) that provide durable agent memory behind an API, handling embeddings, storage, and retrieval. Distinct from self-hosted episodic or file memory by being delivered as a cloud product with SLAs and SDKs.

nodes.label: Personal AI
nodes.short_description: Memory-centric personal assistants that retain per-user lifelong context across sessions and apps.
nodes.long_description: Covers end-user personal AI products built around persistent individual memory — assistants, companions, and second-brain agents. Distinct from generic Memory-as-a-Service (developer APIs); this slot is the consumer-facing product layer that uses such memory.

nodes.label: Assistants
nodes.short_description: Personal AI assistants that maintain long-lived memory about a single user across sessions.
nodes.long_description: User-facing assistants whose core feature is persistent, individualized memory and context rather than generic chat. Distinct from enterprise agents or task runners; these are deployed per-user, often self-hosted, and optimize for continuity over breadth.

nodes.label: Regressive
nodes.short_description: Memory systems that iteratively refine or compress prior context through feedback loops.
nodes.long_description: Experimental slot for context engines that learn from past interactions by regressing over accumulated state (e.g., Agentic Context Engine). Distinct from episodic recall or static stores by actively updating memory representations based on outcomes.

nodes.label: Session State
nodes.short_description: Short-lived per-conversation state stores holding the active working context of a single session.
nodes.long_description: Covers mechanisms for persisting and restoring in-flight agent session data — scratchpads, turn history, transient variables. Distinct from long-term episodic or hosted memory services; this slot's lifetime is bounded by the session.

nodes.label: Skills
nodes.short_description: Reusable skill/procedure memories that agents can load to perform learned tasks on demand.
nodes.long_description: Covers stores of executable or declarative 'skills' — reusable capability packets an agent can recall and apply. Distinct from episodic memory (facts/events) and session state; here the unit of memory is a procedure or competence.

nodes.label: Dev Tools & Skills
nodes.short_description: Tooling, reusable skills, and prompt assets that developers use to build and operate agents.
nodes.long_description: Top-level domain covering engineer-facing artifacts: coding assistants and IDE integrations (Dev Tools), packaged agent capabilities (Skills), and prompt engineering assets (Prompts). Excludes runtimes, frameworks, and infra, which live elsewhere in the taxonomy.

nodes.label: Dev Tools
nodes.short_description: Developer-facing tools for writing, refactoring, debugging, and shipping code with AI assistance.
nodes.long_description: Tools that embed LLMs into the software engineering loop: IDE plugins, autonomous SWE harnesses, refactoring assistants, and debuggers. Sibling to Skills and Prompts, which are reusable assets rather than interactive tools. Delivered as CLIs, IDE extensions, or standalone harnesses.

nodes.label: Coding Agent SWE Harnesses
nodes.short_description: Autonomous software-engineering harnesses that drive an LLM through multi-step code edits, runs, and tests.
nodes.long_description: Agent loops specialized for the SWE task: read repo, plan, edit files, run tests, iterate. Distinct from passive IDE integrations, which keep the developer in the driver's seat, and from debuggers. Delivered as CLIs or local services that take a task and return a patch.

nodes.label: Debugging
nodes.short_description: LLM-assisted debugging tools that help diagnose failing runs, logs, or models.
nodes.long_description: Tools dedicated to fault diagnosis—stack traces, runtime telemetry, or model-internals inspection—using LLMs as the reasoning layer. Sibling to SWE harnesses (edit-loop oriented) and IDE integrations (authoring oriented). Typically invoked on an error artifact rather than a whole repo.

nodes.label: IDE Integrations
nodes.short_description: IDE and editor extensions that embed LLM coding assistance directly into the developer's editor.
nodes.long_description: Plugins for VS Code, JetBrains, and similar editors providing inline completion, chat, and edit actions. Distinct from autonomous SWE harnesses—these keep the human in the loop—and from standalone CLIs. Shipped as editor extensions.

nodes.label: Repo Refactoring
nodes.short_description: Tools that perform repository-wide refactors and structural rewrites using LLM-guided transformations.
nodes.long_description: Focused on large-scale, cross-file code changes—renames, migrations, architectural rewrites—rather than single-file edits or SWE task loops. Sibling to SWE harnesses and IDE plugins; this slot is specifically about refactor-as-a-workflow.

nodes.label: Prompts
nodes.short_description: Reusable prompt assets and tooling for authoring, managing, formatting, and optimizing LLM prompts.
nodes.long_description: Prompt engineering surface covering conversational control, formatting, workflow definition, management, structured output, and compiler-style optimizers. Distinct from Skills (packaged capabilities) and Dev Tools (interactive coding tools); this slot is about the prompt as an artifact.

nodes.label: Conversational Control
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for constraining and steering the conversational behavior of LLM agents via prompt rules.
nodes.long_description: Tools that define guardrails, flows, and allowed turns for conversational agents at the prompt layer. Distinct from formatting or workflow-definition siblings, which target output shape or task DAGs rather than dialog dynamics. Typically a library or DSL wrapping the model call.

nodes.label: Feedback Formatting
nodes.short_description: Shaping user or tool feedback into prompts so models incorporate it reliably on the next turn.
nodes.long_description: Utilities that structure critique, evaluation results, or tool errors into a form the LLM can act on. Distinct from general output Formatting (model-to-user) and Structured Output (schema enforcement); this slot is about the feedback signal flowing back into the prompt.

nodes.label: Formatting
nodes.short_description: Encoding schemes and token-efficient formats for representing data inside prompts.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and specs—like TOON—that define compact or LLM-friendly serializations as alternatives to JSON/YAML in prompts. Distinct from Structured Output (enforcing model output shape) and Feedback Formatting (critique). Focused on how input data is rendered for the model.

nodes.label: Prompt Management
nodes.short_description: Tools for storing, versioning, and organising prompt libraries across projects and teams.
nodes.long_description: Systems that treat prompts as first-class artifacts: CRUD, versioning, tagging, and retrieval of prompt templates. Distinct from optimisation (which rewrites prompts) and formatting (which shapes a single prompt). Typically CLIs, web apps, or SDKs backing a prompt store.

nodes.label: Prompt Optimisation & Compilation
nodes.short_description: Frameworks that automatically tune, rewrite, or compile prompts from higher-level program specifications.
nodes.long_description: Libraries that optimise prompts via gradient-like feedback, bootstrapped demonstrations, or programmatic compilation. Distinct from manual prompt management: the prompt is an output, not an input. Usually Python SDKs wrapping an LLM call with a training loop (DSPy, TextGrad, AdalFlow).

nodes.label: Structured Output
nodes.short_description: Techniques and libraries for forcing LLMs to emit schema-valid structured data.
nodes.long_description: Covers constrained decoding, grammar enforcement, and JSON/Pydantic coercion at inference time. Distinct from formatting (stylistic) and workflow definition (control flow). Typically SDKs that wrap model calls and guarantee parseable output.

nodes.label: Constrained Decoding Libraries
nodes.short_description: Libraries that constrain LLM decoding to a grammar, regex, or schema at token level.
nodes.long_description: Low-level tools that modify sampling to guarantee outputs conform to a formal grammar (JSON Schema, CFG, regex). Examples: Outlines, Guidance, XGrammar, Instructor. Typically Python libraries hooking into local inference runtimes or wrapping provider APIs.

nodes.label: Workflow Definition
nodes.short_description: DSLs and libraries for declaring multi-step prompt workflows and procedures.
nodes.long_description: Lets authors define the shape of a task — steps, branching, roles — as reusable prompt-driven workflows, separate from full agent orchestration frameworks. Usually a markup, YAML, or code-based DSL consumed by a runner.

nodes.label: Skills
nodes.short_description: Packaged, reusable agent skills—capability bundles with guides, registries, and interoperability standards.
nodes.long_description: Everything around 'skills' as distributable units of agent capability: bundle formats, package libraries, registries, authoring guides, and cross-vendor standards. Sibling to Prompts (raw text assets) and Dev Tools (interactive IDE tooling). Think plugin ecosystem for agents.

nodes.label: Bundles
nodes.short_description: Packaged, distributable collections of agent skills shipped as a unit.
nodes.long_description: Bundles group multiple skills into a single installable artifact (e.g. a Claude Code skill pack). Distinct from registries (discovery) and package libraries (individual modules). Typically tarballs or repo layouts consumed by a skill loader.

nodes.label: Claude Code
nodes.short_description: Skill bundles specifically targeting the Claude Code harness.
nodes.long_description: Collections of skills authored against Claude Code's skill format and loader conventions. A vendor-specific slot under Bundles. Typically git repos installable into ~/.claude/skills or a plugin marketplace.

nodes.label: Guides
nodes.short_description: Instructional skills that teach an agent how to do a task rather than expose code.
nodes.long_description: Prose/procedure-first skills loaded into context to shape agent behaviour — e.g. a 'taste' guide. Distinct from package libraries (executable modules) and standards (specs). Usually markdown documents consumed by skill-capable harnesses.

nodes.label: Package Library
nodes.short_description: Hosted libraries of reusable agent skills exposed as installable packages.
nodes.long_description: Provider- or platform-hosted catalogues where individual skills can be imported into an agent project (e.g. Vercel Skills). Distinct from registries (indexes across providers) and bundles (grouped packs). Typically an SDK or web console.

nodes.label: Registries
nodes.short_description: Indexes and directories for discovering published agent skills across sources.
nodes.long_description: Browsable listings or APIs that catalogue skills from many authors and platforms. Distinct from package libraries (a single provider's store) and bundles (packaged collections). Typically static sites, JSON indexes, or lightweight APIs.

nodes.label: Standards
nodes.short_description: Open specifications defining how agent skills are authored, packaged, and loaded.
nodes.long_description: Protocol- and schema-level work on what a skill is — its manifest, invocation contract, and interop. Distinct from registries (discovery) and guides (content). Typically a spec document plus reference implementations (e.g. AgentSkills).

nodes.label: Ecosystem
nodes.short_description: Meta-layer covering non-tool entities in the agentic AI landscape: organisations, destinations, and uncategorised items.
nodes.long_description: Holds actors and places around the ecosystem rather than software. Sits outside the functional taxonomy of frameworks, tools, and runtimes. Used for companies, foundations, conferences, and holding pens for items awaiting classification.

nodes.label: Destinations
nodes.short_description: Physical and virtual destinations in the agentic AI world — conferences, hubs, venues.
nodes.long_description: Places people go to engage with the ecosystem: events, meetups, hubs, and community sites. Distinct from organisations (entities) and tools. Acts as a map of where the field congregates.

nodes.label: Organisations
nodes.short_description: Entities shaping the agentic AI ecosystem: companies, foundations, consortia, and government bodies.
nodes.long_description: Umbrella for the organisational actors behind standards, funding, and policy. Distinct from destinations (venues) and projects (tools). Subdivided by organisation type.

nodes.label: Companies
nodes.short_description: For-profit companies operating in the agentic AI space.
nodes.long_description: Commercial entities building, funding, or selling agentic AI products and services. Distinct from foundations (non-profit) and government bodies. Used to track notable vendors as ecosystem actors, not their individual products.

nodes.label: Foundations
nodes.short_description: Non-profit foundations supporting agentic AI standards, research, or community.
nodes.long_description: Independent non-profits such as the Agentic AI Industry Foundation. Distinct from for-profit companies and multi-party consortia. Tracked as ecosystem stewards rather than tool vendors.

nodes.label: Foundations & Consortia
nodes.short_description: Multi-member consortia and joint foundations coordinating agentic AI standards work.
nodes.long_description: Alliances bringing multiple organisations together around shared specs, protocols, or governance. Distinct from single-entity foundations and private companies. Typically publish specifications and reference implementations.

nodes.label: Government Bodies
nodes.short_description: Government and public-sector bodies with roles in agentic AI policy or oversight.
nodes.long_description: Regulators, agencies, and state-affiliated institutes shaping how agents are deployed and governed. Distinct from private companies and independent foundations. Tracked for their policy and standards influence.

nodes.label: Uncategorized
nodes.short_description: Holding pen for items awaiting classification into the taxonomy.
nodes.long_description: Temporary bucket for projects or entities not yet assigned a functional slot. Should trend toward empty as the taxonomy is refined. Not a stable category.

nodes.label: Frameworks & Runtimes
nodes.short_description: Top-level domain for the software substrate that executes agents: frameworks, runtimes, harnesses, loops, and orchestration.
nodes.long_description: Covers everything an agent runs on or is built with — from authoring frameworks and step loops to orchestration engines, runtimes, and starter kits. Distinct from tools (capabilities the agent calls) and models. The plumbing layer of the stack.

nodes.label: Environments
nodes.short_description: Execution environments where agents run — operating systems, desktops, and other host surfaces distinct from the framework code itself.
nodes.long_description: Covers the substrate an agent inhabits: OS-level agent shells, remote desktop hosts, and similar runtime surfaces. Distinct from Runtimes (process/sandbox layer) and Frameworks (programming model); an environment is the place the agent perceives and acts within.

nodes.label: OS
nodes.short_description: Operating-system-style environments purpose-built as agent hosts, where the OS itself exposes primitives for agent perception and action.
nodes.long_description: OS-level environments that treat the agent as a first-class citizen — shell, filesystem, and process model shaped around agent use. Distinct from remote desktops (which reuse a standard desktop) in that the OS itself is the abstraction.

nodes.label: Remote Desktops
nodes.short_description: Hosted remote desktop environments that agents drive via screen/keyboard/mouse — cloud VMs packaged as agent workspaces.
nodes.long_description: Remote desktops provisioned for computer-use agents: a real OS GUI streamed over the network that an agent controls. Distinct from OS-level agent environments (custom substrates) — here the desktop is standard and the contribution is the hosting, isolation, and streaming layer.

nodes.label: Frameworks
nodes.short_description: Programming-model libraries and SDKs for building agents — the code-facing abstractions developers import to define agent behavior.
nodes.long_description: Libraries, SDKs, and DSLs that provide the primitives (agents, tools, memory, loops) for authoring agentic systems. Distinct from Runtimes (execution), Orchestration (multi-agent coordination services), and Harnesses (wrappers around existing agents). Subdivided by language, modality, and design style.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Frameworks specialized for computer-use agents — primitives for screen observation, GUI control, and desktop task loops.
nodes.long_description: Libraries whose abstractions are shaped around driving a computer (screenshots, clicks, keystrokes, UI grounding). Distinct from general multi-agent frameworks and from computer-use models themselves; this is the developer-facing scaffolding for building such agents.

nodes.label: Multiagent Orchestration
nodes.short_description: Frameworks whose core abstraction is a team of cooperating agents — roles, messaging, and group workflows as first-class primitives.
nodes.long_description: Libraries built around multi-agent collaboration patterns: role assignment, inter-agent messaging, task delegation. Distinct from the Orchestration segment (standalone coordination services) — these are developer frameworks you import to define the agents and their interactions in code.

nodes.label: Multimodal
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for agents that natively handle multiple modalities — video, audio, and vision alongside text.
nodes.long_description: Agent libraries where multimodal I/O (video, audio, image streams) is a first-class concern rather than an afterthought. Distinct from voice-only or text-only frameworks; typical interface is an SDK with streaming media primitives wired into the agent loop.

nodes.label: .NET
nodes.short_description: .NET-ecosystem agent frameworks — C#/F# libraries for building agents on the .NET runtime.
nodes.long_description: Agent frameworks targeting the .NET platform, providing idiomatic C#/F# APIs for agents, tools, and memory. Sibling to language/modality-specific framework slots; grouped by host language because ecosystem conventions and package distribution differ meaningfully from Python/TypeScript.

nodes.label: Optimisation
nodes.short_description: Frameworks focused on optimising agent execution — parallelisation, batching, and runtime efficiency of agent workloads.
nodes.long_description: Libraries whose contribution is making agent systems faster or cheaper to run: parallel execution of sub-tasks, batched tool calls, scheduling tricks. Distinct from Orchestration (routing) — optimisation frameworks shape how individual agent workloads execute, not how multiple agents coordinate.

nodes.label: Parallelisation
nodes.short_description: Framework-level optimisation techniques for running agent workloads in parallel to cut latency or cost.
nodes.long_description: Parallelisation primitives inside agent frameworks — concurrent tool calls, fan-out/fan-in execution, parallel chain steps. Complements other optimisation strategies (batching, caching). Exposed as framework APIs or execution planners.

nodes.label: Social Simulations
nodes.short_description: Frameworks for simulating populations of agents in social settings — generative agents, games, and behavioral experiments.
nodes.long_description: Libraries designed to instantiate many interacting agents for sociological, economic, or behavioral simulation. Distinct from multi-agent orchestration (which targets task completion): the deliverable is the simulated society itself, often for research or game design.

nodes.label: Stateful Design
nodes.short_description: Frameworks that treat persistent agent state and memory as the central design concern — long-lived agents with durable identity.
nodes.long_description: Libraries organized around stateful agents: memory hierarchies, self-editing context, and long-term persistence are first-class. Distinct from stateless chain-of-tool frameworks; typical interface is a server plus SDK where agents outlive a single request and accumulate memory across sessions.

nodes.label: Voice
nodes.short_description: Voice-first agent frameworks — libraries built around realtime speech I/O, turn-taking, and telephony-style interaction.
nodes.long_description: Frameworks whose primary modality is voice: streaming STT/TTS, barge-in, realtime pipelines, and telephony integration. Distinct from generic multimodal frameworks by their realtime-voice focus. Typical interface: an SDK plus a media server coordinating audio streams with LLM turns.

nodes.label: Realtime Voice Pipelines
nodes.short_description: Frameworks wiring STT, LLM, and TTS into low-latency duplex pipelines for conversational voice agents.
nodes.long_description: Toolkits for building realtime voice agents that stream audio in and out with interruption handling and turn-taking. Distinct from generic voice SDKs by orchestrating the full pipeline. Typically Python/Node libraries or managed services.

nodes.label: Harnesses
nodes.short_description: Wrappers that extend or control an existing agent/LLM loop — adding research, dispatch, checkpointing, recovery, or compression around it.
nodes.long_description: Harnesses sit around a base agent or model and add cross-cutting behaviors: long-horizon research, sub-agent dispatch, context compression, retry and recovery, checkpointing. Distinct from Frameworks (you build with) and Runtimes (you run on); a harness is what you bolt onto a model or agent to make it durable and task-coherent.

nodes.label: Checkpointing
nodes.short_description: Harness features that snapshot agent state mid-run so tasks can be paused, resumed, or branched without replaying from scratch.
nodes.long_description: Mechanisms for capturing and restoring an agent's working state — memory, scratchpad, tool history — at arbitrary points in a long task. Distinct from retry/recovery (which handles failure) and context compression (which shrinks state); checkpointing is about durable resumability.

nodes.label: Context Compression
nodes.short_description: Harness techniques that shrink an agent's growing context window — summarisation, pruning, and hierarchical memory for long runs.
nodes.long_description: Strategies and tools for keeping context usable as an agent accumulates history: rolling summaries, selective eviction, hierarchical notes. Distinct from general memory systems — this sits inside the agent loop to keep a single run within budget, rather than providing cross-session memory.

nodes.label: Deep Research
nodes.short_description: Harnesses specialised for long-horizon, multi-step research tasks with iterative search, reading, and synthesis.
nodes.long_description: Agent scaffolds that drive deep-research workflows: planning queries, fanning out to retrieval, reading sources, and composing reports. Distinct from generic sub-agent dispatch in that the harness is opinionated around research loops. Typically a library or runtime template layered over an LLM.

nodes.label: Platform Specific
nodes.short_description: Agent harnesses tightly coupled to a specific host OS or platform rather than being runtime-agnostic.
nodes.long_description: Harnesses whose design assumes a particular platform (e.g. macOS, iOS, Windows) for native APIs, sandboxing, or UI control. In contrast to cross-platform harnesses, these trade portability for deep platform integration. Usually shipped as native apps or platform SDKs.

nodes.label: macOS
nodes.short_description: Agent harnesses built specifically for macOS, leveraging native APIs and Apple silicon integration.
nodes.long_description: Harnesses that target macOS as a first-class host — using AppKit, Accessibility, or Metal/MLX for local inference and control. Shipped as native apps or Mac-only binaries. Sibling slot to other OS-specific harnesses under Platform Specific.

nodes.label: Retry and Recovery
nodes.short_description: Harness components handling failure detection, retry policy, and recovery from tool or model errors mid-task.
nodes.long_description: Mechanisms that let an agent survive transient tool failures, malformed outputs, or stalled reasoning — backoff, re-planning, fallback paths. Sibling to checkpointing (which persists state) but focused on in-loop error handling. Exposed as harness middleware or runtime policies.

nodes.label: Sub-agent Dispatch
nodes.short_description: Harness logic for spawning, routing to, and collecting results from sub-agents under a parent controller.
nodes.long_description: Covers the dispatch layer that lets a primary agent delegate work to specialised sub-agents and merge their outputs. Distinct from general multi-agent orchestration in that it lives inside a single harness as a control primitive. Usually a library API for task fan-out.

nodes.label: Task Coherence
nodes.short_description: Harness features that keep an agent on-task across long runs, preventing drift and goal decay.
nodes.long_description: Techniques for maintaining alignment between an agent's current actions and its original goal — goal reminders, plan re-grounding, scope guards. Complements context compression (which shrinks history) by ensuring intent survives the compression. Typically library-level scaffolding.

nodes.label: Loops
nodes.short_description: Minimal agent loop patterns — tight read-think-act cycles exposed as the primary abstraction rather than a full framework.
nodes.long_description: Projects whose central contribution is the iteration loop itself: a small, opinionated control flow that repeatedly prompts, acts, and observes. Distinct from Frameworks (which bundle many concerns) and Harnesses (which wrap existing agents). Useful as building blocks or reference implementations.

nodes.label: Orchestration
nodes.short_description: Coordination layers that route tasks across multiple agents, models, or workers — the conductor rather than the individual performer.
nodes.long_description: Services and libraries that dispatch, schedule, and compose work across many agents or model endpoints. Distinct from multi-agent frameworks (which define the agents) in that orchestration focuses on routing, policy, and cross-agent flow. Typical interface: a controller service or SDK that agents and tools register with.

nodes.label: Platform-Specific
nodes.short_description: Orchestration frameworks tied to a specific cloud, vendor, or platform ecosystem.
nodes.long_description: Multi-agent orchestration offerings that assume a particular provider (e.g. AWS, Azure) for routing, state, and tool integration. Distinct from vendor-neutral orchestrators. Usually delivered as SDKs or managed services bound to that platform's primitives.

nodes.label: Pipelines
nodes.short_description: Declarative, staged data/agent pipelines — DAG-style flows that transform inputs into agent-driven outputs.
nodes.long_description: Pipeline-oriented tooling for chaining extractors, transformers, and agent stages into a repeatable flow. Distinct from Orchestration (dynamic multi-agent routing) and Loops (tight iteration): pipelines emphasize staged, mostly linear transformations, often to construct agents or artifacts from source material.

nodes.label: Runtimes
nodes.short_description: Execution substrates that host agent processes — sandboxes, policy engines, and data layers that enforce isolation and lifecycle.
nodes.long_description: Runtime layers providing the OS-like concerns for agents: sandboxing, policy enforcement, persistent data. Distinct from Frameworks (developer API) and Environments (the surface the agent acts on); the runtime is what actually runs the agent process and governs what it can touch.

nodes.label: Data Layers
nodes.short_description: Runtime-adjacent storage and data-access layers that agents rely on for state, memory, and dataset I/O.
nodes.long_description: Data substrates embedded in or paired with agent runtimes — versioned datasets, vector-ready lakes, append-only logs. Sibling to sandboxes (execution) and policy engines (authorisation). Usually a library or service exposing a dataset or storage API to running agents.

nodes.label: Policy Engines
nodes.short_description: Runtime components that evaluate and enforce permission, safety, or governance policies on agent actions.
nodes.long_description: Authorisation and guardrail engines invoked by an agent runtime before tool calls or state mutations. Distinct from data layers and sandboxes in that they answer allow/deny questions against declared rules. Typically embedded libraries or sidecar services.

nodes.label: Sandboxes
nodes.short_description: Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, shell commands, or untrusted tools safely.
nodes.long_description: Ephemeral, isolated compute for agent-driven actions, ranging from microVMs to container-based sandboxes. Distinct from policy engines (rules) and data layers (state) by providing an execution surface. Delivered as APIs, services, or embeddable runtimes.

nodes.label: Code Execution Sandboxes
nodes.short_description: Sandboxes purpose-built for running LLM-generated code with fast startup and per-session isolation.
nodes.long_description: Managed or self-hosted services that spin up disposable VMs/containers for each agent code-execution request, with filesystem, network, and package tooling. Distinct from general sandboxes by optimising for code interpreters. Consumed via SDKs and REST APIs.

nodes.label: Starter Kits
nodes.short_description: Opinionated scaffolds and templates for bootstrapping a new agent project — boilerplate plus conventions, not a framework.
nodes.long_description: Ready-to-clone templates, reference apps, and scaffolds that get a developer from zero to a running agent quickly. Distinct from Frameworks (the library they wrap) in that a starter kit is a project skeleton, often specialized by domain (e.g. social simulations).

nodes.label: Social Simulations
nodes.short_description: Starter kits for building multi-agent social simulations with personas, worlds, and interactions.
nodes.long_description: Opinionated templates for populating a simulated environment with many agents that socialise, plan, and act — à la generative agents research. Distinct from general multi-agent orchestration by providing world, persona, and UI scaffolding. Usually a runnable sample project.

nodes.label: Interfaces
nodes.short_description: User-facing surfaces through which humans interact with agents — frontends, workspaces, UX patterns, and annotation tools.
nodes.long_description: Top-level domain covering everything a human sees or touches when working with agents: client apps, chat UIs, workspace environments, UX primitives like human-in-the-loop, and data annotation interfaces. Sits above the runtime and framework layers.

nodes.label: Annotation
nodes.short_description: Tools for labelling, annotating, and reviewing agent data — traces, outputs, training examples.
nodes.long_description: Interfaces where humans add labels, corrections, or judgements to agent inputs and outputs. Used for dataset curation, eval scoring, and RLHF-style feedback. Distinct from workspaces and frontends by being editorial rather than conversational.

nodes.label: Frontends
nodes.short_description: Client applications that expose agent capabilities to end users across desktop, mobile, web, and terminal.
nodes.long_description: End-user client software — chat apps, desktop clients, mobile apps, TUIs, web UIs — that front an agent backend. Distinct from workspaces (multi-agent environments) and UX primitives. Usually a shipped binary or web app talking to model/agent APIs.

nodes.label: Android
nodes.short_description: Android-native client apps for interacting with agents and LLMs on mobile.
nodes.long_description: Frontends packaged as Android applications, using native OS capabilities for notifications, background tasks, and device integration. Distinct from cross-platform mobile clients by targeting Android specifically. Distributed via APK or Play Store.

nodes.label: Chat UIs
nodes.short_description: Chat-style frontends that render agent conversations as threaded message UIs.
nodes.long_description: Reusable chat interface components and apps designed specifically for agent interactions — message streams, tool-call rendering, and thread history. Distinct from platform-specific desktop/mobile/web clients: focuses on the chat UX paradigm itself, often as an embeddable library or shell over an underlying agent runtime.

nodes.label: Desktop Clients
nodes.short_description: Native or packaged desktop applications for interacting with agents on Windows, macOS, or Linux.
nodes.long_description: Installable desktop apps exposing agent chat, tools, and workflows through an OS-native shell. Unlike web clients, they run locally with filesystem and OS integration; subdivided by target platform (Windows, macOS, Linux, Cross-Platform).

nodes.label: Cross-Platform
nodes.short_description: Desktop agent clients built to run on Windows, macOS, and Linux from a single codebase.
nodes.long_description: Electron/Tauri/Qt-style desktop apps targeting all three major OSes. Distinguished from per-platform siblings by a shared codebase and unified feature parity across operating systems.

nodes.label: Linux
nodes.short_description: Linux-native or Linux-first desktop clients for agents.
nodes.long_description: Desktop agent applications specifically packaged for Linux — often via Flatpak, AppImage, or distro repos — or taking advantage of Linux-specific integrations. Distinct from cross-platform builds by being Linux-targeted primary releases.

nodes.label: macOS
nodes.short_description: macOS-native desktop clients for agents.
nodes.long_description: Agent desktop apps built for macOS, often leveraging Apple frameworks and shipped via DMG or the App Store. Distinguished from cross-platform builds by being Mac-only or Mac-first.

nodes.label: Windows
nodes.short_description: Windows-native desktop clients for agents.
nodes.long_description: Agent desktop apps distributed as Windows installers (MSI/EXE/Store) with Windows-specific integrations. Separated from cross-platform builds to capture tools that are Windows-only or Windows-first.

nodes.label: GUIs and CLIs
nodes.short_description: Hybrid frontends exposing the same agent through both a graphical interface and a command-line entry point.
nodes.long_description: Tools that ship a GUI alongside a CLI, letting users drive the same agent interactively or from scripts. Distinct from pure TUI/terminal clients: here the GUI is a first-class surface, not an afterthought.

nodes.label: Mobile Clients
nodes.short_description: Mobile apps (iOS/Android) for interacting with agents from phones and tablets.
nodes.long_description: Native or hybrid mobile applications exposing agent chat and tools on handheld devices. Separated from desktop and web clients because mobile constraints (touch, push, background) shape the UX; subdivided by platform.

nodes.label: Android
nodes.short_description: Android apps for interacting with agents.
nodes.long_description: Agent clients packaged as APKs/AABs for Android phones and tablets, distributed via Play Store or sideload. Distinct from iOS siblings by platform SDK, distribution, and integration surface.

nodes.label: iOS
nodes.short_description: iOS apps for interacting with agents on iPhone and iPad.
