nodes.long_description: Agent clients built with Apple's iOS SDKs and distributed via the App Store or TestFlight. Separated from Android siblings by platform, distribution, and sandboxing model.

nodes.label: Server Software
nodes.short_description: Self-hostable server backends that power agent frontends with multi-user state, auth, and persistence.
nodes.long_description: Backend services that sit behind chat/web/desktop clients, providing accounts, conversation storage, model routing, and tool access. Unlike inference servers, their job is frontend-facing session and UX state, not raw model execution.

nodes.label: Terminal Clients
nodes.short_description: Terminal-resident clients that expose agents as a shell-style REPL or command.
nodes.long_description: Lightweight terminal programs for chatting with or dispatching agents from the command line. Simpler than full TUIs (no panels/keybindings) and distinct from GUI/desktop clients — the terminal is the entire surface.

nodes.label: TUIs and CLIs
nodes.short_description: Text-based interactive frontends combining terminal UIs with command-line invocation.
nodes.long_description: Interactive TUI dashboards plus scriptable CLI entry points for agents, running fully inside a terminal. Distinct from pure terminal clients by offering richer panel/keybinding UIs, and from GUI+CLI hybrids by staying text-only.

nodes.label: Web Clients
nodes.short_description: Browser-based frontends for agents, served as web apps or SPAs.
nodes.long_description: Hosted or self-hostable web applications providing chat and tool UIs to agents over HTTP. Distinct from desktop clients (no install) and from server software (which powers them): this slot is the browser-facing UI layer.

nodes.label: UX
nodes.short_description: Reusable UX patterns and primitives for agent interaction, such as human-in-the-loop approvals and inspection surfaces.
nodes.long_description: Design patterns and component libraries that shape how users supervise, approve, or correct agent actions. Distinct from full frontends by being pattern-level rather than application-level. Often delivered as UI libraries or reference implementations.

nodes.label: Human in the Loop
nodes.short_description: UX patterns and inboxes letting humans review, approve, or correct agent actions mid-run.
nodes.long_description: Tools providing approval queues, intervention points, and review inboxes where an agent pauses for human judgment before proceeding. Typically integrates with an orchestrator via webhooks or SDK callbacks; distinct from generic chat UIs by centering on gating and oversight workflows.

nodes.label: Workspaces
nodes.short_description: Multi-agent workspace environments where users manage, converse with, and orchestrate several agents in one place.
nodes.long_description: Integrated environments that host multiple agents, conversations, and tools behind a unified UI — closer to an IDE or team workspace than a single chat client. Distinct from frontends (single-client) by being multi-agent and multi-session. Typically web apps or desktop workspaces.

nodes.label: Models & Inference
nodes.short_description: The model layer: base models themselves and the infrastructure that serves them to agents.
nodes.long_description: Top-level segment covering both the models an agent calls (LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, synthetic data) and the inference plumbing around them (servers, gateways, routers, caches). Distinct from frameworks and tooling: this is where tokens are generated and routed, not where agent logic is authored.

nodes.label: Inference
nodes.short_description: Infrastructure that serves, routes, and optimises model calls between agents and model backends.
nodes.long_description: The serving plane: inference servers, gateways, proxies, routers, semantic caches, and structured-output enforcement that sit between an agent and the underlying model. Distinct from the Models sibling, which covers the models themselves rather than how requests reach them.

nodes.label: Gateways
nodes.short_description: Unified API gateways that front multiple model providers behind a single endpoint.
nodes.long_description: Services exposing one OpenAI-compatible (or similar) API that fans out to many providers, applying auth, quota, logging, and context compression. Distinct from routers (which pick a model per request) and proxies (which are thinner pass-throughs) by offering a full management plane.

nodes.label: Context Compression
nodes.short_description: Gateway-layer services that compress or prune context windows before requests reach the model.
nodes.long_description: Middleware that shrinks prompts — via summarization, dedup, or token pruning — to fit budgets and cut cost. Deployed as an inline gateway component. Distinct from memory stores and from model-side KV compression.

nodes.label: Inference Servers
nodes.short_description: Servers that execute model inference locally or on dedicated hardware.
nodes.long_description: Runtimes such as vLLM-style engines that load model weights and expose an HTTP/gRPC inference API. Distinct from gateways/routers (which forward to other backends) — inference servers actually run the model and generate tokens.

nodes.label: Proxy
nodes.short_description: Transparent proxies that sit between agents and model APIs, forwarding requests while adapting protocols or endpoints.
nodes.long_description: Covers proxy-style middleware for LLM traffic: protocol bridging (e.g. MCP to OpenAI), endpoint translation, and drop-in API compatibility layers. Distinct from Gateways (policy/auth), Routers (model selection), and Inference Servers (actual execution).

nodes.label: Gateways
nodes.short_description: Unified LLM gateway proxies exposing many providers behind one API with auth, logging, and rate limiting.
nodes.long_description: Covers multi-provider proxies like LiteLLM and AgentGateway that normalize provider APIs and add ops features. Sit in front of all model traffic for an agent or org. Distinct from pure routers (decision logic) and protocol bridges.

nodes.label: MCP to OpenAI
nodes.short_description: Bridges that expose MCP servers as OpenAI-compatible tool endpoints.
nodes.long_description: Proxies such as MCPO that translate between the Model Context Protocol and the OpenAI tool/function-calling API, letting OpenAI-style clients consume MCP tools. Distinct from generic LLM gateways.

nodes.label: Routers
nodes.short_description: Request routers that pick which model or provider should handle a given prompt based on cost, capability, or tool affinity.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and services that decide, per-request, which backend LLM to call. Typically exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint and dispatches based on heuristics, classifiers, or policies. Distinct from Gateways (auth/observability) and Proxies (protocol translation).

nodes.label: Cost Optimisation
nodes.short_description: Routers whose primary objective is minimising spend by dispatching to the cheapest adequate model or provider.
nodes.long_description: Cost-aware routing layers that track per-token pricing and redirect traffic to cheaper backends when quality allows. Distinct from intelligent (capability-based) and tool-specific routers.

nodes.label: Intelligent
nodes.short_description: Capability-aware routers that classify prompts and dispatch to the best-suited model.
nodes.long_description: Semantic or learned routers (Semantic Router, Agentic Flow) that inspect intent or difficulty and pick a model accordingly. Distinct from cost-first and tool-specific routing.

nodes.label: Tool Specific
nodes.short_description: Routers specialised for dispatching tool calls to the model best suited to execute them.
nodes.long_description: Routing layers that look at the tool-use surface of a request and pick a model with strong performance for that tool or schema. Distinct from general capability or cost routers.

nodes.label: Semantic Caching
nodes.short_description: Caches that reuse prior LLM responses when a new prompt is semantically similar to one already seen.
nodes.long_description: Middleware that embeds prompts and returns cached completions on vector-similarity hits, cutting cost and latency. Usually deployed as a proxy or SDK in front of the model. Distinct from generic HTTP caches and from KV-cache reuse inside inference servers.

nodes.label: Structured Output Gateways
nodes.short_description: Gateways that enforce typed, schema-valid outputs from LLMs via constrained decoding, grammars, or retry loops.
nodes.long_description: Tools like BAML and TensorZero that wrap model calls to guarantee JSON/DSL-conformant responses, handling parsing, validation, and repair. Sit between the agent and the raw inference endpoint. Distinct from routing or caching gateways focused on dispatch or reuse.

nodes.label: Models
nodes.short_description: The models themselves and tools for producing them — LLMs, embeddings, trainers, RL, and synthetic data.
nodes.long_description: Covers base and fine-tuned models an agent consumes (LLMs, embedding models) plus the pipelines that create them (trainers, RL, synthetic data generation). Sibling of Inference, which handles serving; this slot is about what gets served.

nodes.label: Embedding Models
nodes.short_description: Embedding models that turn text (or other modalities) into vectors for retrieval, clustering, and semantic search.
nodes.long_description: Covers released embedding models used by agents for RAG, memory, and similarity search. Distinct from LLMs (generative) and from the databases or pipelines that consume the vectors.

nodes.label: LLMs
nodes.short_description: Generative large language models used as the reasoning core of agents.
nodes.long_description: Frontier and open LLM releases positioned for agentic use, including variants tuned for specific use cases or agent workflows. Distinct from embedding models, RL policies, and the tooling that trains or serves them.

nodes.label: Agent Specific
nodes.short_description: LLMs purpose-built or tuned for agentic workflows — planning, tool use, multi-agent coordination.
nodes.long_description: Models released with agent scenarios as the primary target, e.g. multi-agent variants of frontier models. Distinct from general-purpose LLMs grouped by use case.

nodes.label: By Use Case
nodes.short_description: LLMs grouped by the specific downstream use case they are optimised for.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for LLMs released or tuned for particular task families (computer use, coding, etc.). Distinct from agent-specific models where agency itself is the target.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: LLMs tuned specifically for computer-use agents that control a GUI, browser, or desktop.
nodes.long_description: Models such as Fara released for screen-grounded action prediction and desktop/browser automation. Distinct from general vision-language models and from the harnesses that execute the actions.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning
nodes.short_description: RL frameworks, algorithms, and environments used to train or post-train agentic models.
nodes.long_description: Covers RL libraries (Acme-style) and agent-focused RL runtimes (RLinf), plus environment suites for agent training. Distinct from supervised trainers and from the models themselves; sits upstream of deployed agents.

nodes.label: Agent RL Environments
nodes.short_description: Environment suites and harnesses for training and evaluating agents via reinforcement learning.
nodes.long_description: Projects like SkyRL, Verifiers, and OpenEnv that provide standardized agent environments, reward signals, and rollout infrastructure. Distinct from RL algorithms themselves and from eval benchmarks used purely for scoring.

nodes.label: Synthetic Data
nodes.short_description: Pipelines and tools for generating synthetic training or eval data for agent models.
nodes.long_description: Covers projects that produce LLM-generated datasets — instructions, trajectories, tool-use traces — used to fine-tune or evaluate agents. Distinct from trainers (which consume the data) and from eval frameworks (which score models).

nodes.label: Trainers
nodes.short_description: Training and fine-tuning frameworks aimed at producing agent-capable models.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and services for SFT, DPO, and agent-specific fine-tuning (e.g. tool-use). Covers end-to-end trainers like Agent Lightning. Distinct from RL frameworks and from hosted inference; sits between raw data and deployable checkpoints.

nodes.label: Tool-Use Fine-Tuning
nodes.short_description: Fine-tuning services and pipelines specialised in teaching models to call tools and APIs correctly.
nodes.long_description: Covers OpenPipe, Together fine-tuning, and similar offerings focused on tool-call trajectories and function-calling accuracy. Distinct from generic SFT trainers and from RL environments.

nodes.label: Observability & Evals
nodes.short_description: Telemetry, evaluation, and operational monitoring for LLM and agent systems across their lifecycle.
nodes.long_description: Covers benchmarks, eval and experiment-tracking frameworks, runtime observability, and cost/ops monitoring for agents and LLM applications. In scope: tracing, judge frameworks, analytics, SRE-style tooling. Out of scope: safety policy enforcement (see Safety & Governance) and model training infra.

nodes.label: Agent Analytics
nodes.short_description: Product analytics tailored to agent behavior — task success, tool usage, session funnels, user interactions.
nodes.long_description: Dashboards and metrics pipelines that treat agent runs as the unit of analysis, surfacing trends in completion rates, tool calls, latency, and drop-off. Distinct from raw observability (traces) and evals (scored runs): the focus is aggregate product/usage insight over time.

nodes.label: Benchmarks
nodes.short_description: Standardized task suites and datasets used to score agent or LLM capability under controlled conditions.
nodes.long_description: Parent slot for benchmark harnesses and datasets — software engineering, web navigation, general assistants, and more. Typical interface: a task runner plus grading script that produces a comparable score. Distinct from eval frameworks, which build custom in-house test sets.

nodes.label: General Assistant Benchmarks
nodes.short_description: Benchmarks measuring broad, open-ended assistant capability across mixed reasoning, tool-use, and knowledge tasks.
nodes.long_description: Task suites like GAIA and AgentBench that probe general-purpose agent competence rather than a narrow vertical. Typically scored end-to-end on diverse real-world questions. Sibling to SWE and web-agent benchmarks, which focus on specific domains.

nodes.label: Software Engineering Benchmarks
nodes.short_description: Benchmarks grading agents on code tasks — bug fixing, PR resolution, terminal workflows, freelance-style tickets.
nodes.long_description: Includes SWE-bench-style repo tasks, terminal-based shell tasks, and freelance/contract coding challenges. Typical interface: a dockerized repo plus test suite used as pass/fail oracle. Distinct from web-agent and general-assistant benchmarks.

nodes.label: Web Agent Benchmarks
nodes.short_description: Benchmarks that evaluate agents on browser and web navigation tasks in realistic or simulated sites.
nodes.long_description: Harnesses like WebArena, VisualWebArena and Mind2Web that score agents on multi-step tasks across reproducible web environments. Typical interface: a sandboxed web stack plus task specs. Distinct from OS-level computer-use benchmarks and SWE benchmarks.

nodes.label: Evals and Experiment Tracking
nodes.short_description: Frameworks and services for running evals, tracing agent runs, and tracking experiments across model/prompt versions.
nodes.long_description: Covers eval harnesses, LLM-as-judge runners, observability/tracing backends, time-travel debugging, and synthetic user simulators. Typical interface: SDKs that log runs plus a UI for comparison. Sits above raw logging and below product analytics.

nodes.label: Frameworks
nodes.short_description: General-purpose eval frameworks for authoring, running, and scoring test suites against LLM and agent outputs.
nodes.long_description: Libraries such as DeepEval that provide assertions, metrics, and dataset runners for LLM/agent testing. Typical interface: pytest-like SDK or CLI. Distinct from LLM-as-judge frameworks (judge-specific) and observability (trace-centric).

nodes.label: LLM-as-Judge Frameworks
nodes.short_description: Frameworks that use an LLM to grade outputs of another LLM or agent against rubrics or reference answers.
nodes.long_description: Tools like Promptfoo, Inspect AI, and Patronus that orchestrate judge prompts, rubrics, and scoring pipelines. Typical interface: YAML/DSL tests plus a judge model. Distinct from generic eval frameworks by centering on model-graded scoring.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.short_description: Tracing and telemetry backends that capture LLM/agent spans, prompts, and tool calls for live debugging.
nodes.long_description: Services like Langfuse that ingest structured traces from SDK instrumentation and expose a UI for run inspection, filtering, and drill-down. Distinct from eval frameworks (scoring) and time-travel debuggers (replay).

nodes.label: Synthetic User Simulation
nodes.short_description: Tools that generate simulated users to exercise agents in multi-turn conversational or task scenarios.
nodes.long_description: Harnesses such as Tau-bench and Snowglobe that script or LLM-drive fake users for stress testing, regression, and red-teaming of conversational agents. Distinct from static benchmarks by producing dynamic, interactive dialogues.

nodes.label: Trace Replay & Time-Travel Debugging
nodes.short_description: Tools that replay captured agent traces step-by-step, letting developers branch, rewind, and re-run from any point.
nodes.long_description: Services like Braintrust, AgentOps Replay, and LangSmith that turn stored runs into interactive debug sessions — edit a prompt mid-trace and re-execute. Distinct from passive observability by enabling intervention and what-if replay.

nodes.label: Operations
nodes.short_description: Day-two operational tooling for running agent and LLM systems in production — cost, budgets, SLOs.
nodes.long_description: Ops-facing concerns beyond eval quality: spend tracking, rate-limit management, SLO monitoring, on-call signals. Currently anchored by cost monitoring. Distinct from observability (traces/spans) and analytics (usage trends).

nodes.label: Cost Monitoring
nodes.short_description: Tracking token spend, API costs, and budget alerts across models, agents, and tenants.
nodes.long_description: Tools like AgentOps that attribute LLM/API cost to runs, users, or features and raise alerts on budget breaches. Typical interface: SDK hooks plus a billing dashboard. Distinct from general observability by centering on dollars and quotas.

nodes.label: Safety & Governance
nodes.short_description: Controls, policies, and oversight mechanisms that keep agents safe, compliant, and accountable in production.
nodes.long_description: Covers security, identity/auth, PII redaction, guardrails, and human- or agent-in-the-loop oversight for LLM-driven systems. Typical interface: middleware, policy engines, or review UIs. Distinct from observability: focus is enforcement and intervention, not measurement.

nodes.label: Agent in the Loop
nodes.short_description: Patterns where a supervisory agent reviews, approves, or moderates another agent's actions before execution.
nodes.long_description: Architectures that insert an LLM reviewer — a safety critic or policy agent — into another agent's loop to approve tool calls or outputs. Distinct from human-in-the-loop (which routes to people) and static guardrails (which apply rules without reasoning).

nodes.label: Guardrails
nodes.short_description: Runtime filters and policy checks that constrain agent inputs, outputs, or actions against defined rules.
nodes.long_description: Includes input/output validators, refusal classifiers, command confirmation layers like Shellfirm, and policy routers. Typical interface: middleware wrapping model calls or tool invocations. Distinct from identity/auth and PII redaction, which solve narrower problems.

nodes.label: Policy Engines & Guardrail Routers
nodes.short_description: Libraries and routers that enforce content, safety, and behavioral policies on LLM inputs and outputs.
nodes.long_description: Policy engines intercept prompts and responses to block unsafe content, enforce topic boundaries, and route based on rules. Typically embedded as a library or proxy in front of model calls (e.g. NeMo Guardrails, Llama Guard, Guardrails AI). Sits between the agent runtime and the model.

nodes.label: Human in the Loop
nodes.short_description: Workflows and UIs that route agent decisions to humans for approval, correction, or escalation.
nodes.long_description: Review queues, approval gates, and annotator interfaces where a human signs off on risky agent actions or labels ambiguous cases. Distinct from agent-in-the-loop (LLM reviewer) and from pure HITL data labeling, which targets training rather than runtime oversight.

nodes.label: Identity and Auth
nodes.short_description: Identity, authentication, and delegated-authority mechanisms for agents acting on behalf of users or services.
nodes.long_description: Covers agent identity protocols, scoped credentials, OAuth flows for agents, and non-human identity management. Typical interface: identity providers, token brokers, or standards. Distinct from guardrails (content policy) and from generic IAM by focusing on agent-specific delegation.

nodes.label: Agent Identity Protocols
nodes.short_description: Protocols establishing verifiable identity, credentials, and authority for autonomous agents acting on behalf of users.
nodes.long_description: Open specifications for how agents authenticate themselves to services, carry delegated authority, and prove provenance — e.g. agent passports, agent payment protocols. Protocol-level standards, not SDKs; the plumbing for agent-to-service trust across organizational boundaries.

nodes.label: PII and Redaction
nodes.short_description: Detecting and redacting personal, sensitive, or regulated data from prompts, traces, and agent outputs.
nodes.long_description: Tools and libraries that scan text for PII/PHI/PCI and mask or tokenize before it reaches models or logs. Typical interface: middleware or SDK filters. Distinct from general guardrails by targeting data privacy and compliance specifically.

nodes.label: Security
nodes.short_description: Security tooling for agent systems — sandboxing, red-teaming, control planes, and automated defense.
nodes.long_description: Covers the runtime and operational security layer for agents: isolating execution, detecting malicious inputs, enforcing policy at the control plane, and automating incident response. Distinct from Guardrails (content/policy filtering) and Identity (who the agent is) — this slot is about protecting the system from and around the agent.

nodes.label: Automation
nodes.short_description: Security automation and SOAR-style platforms adapted for agent workflows and incident response.
nodes.long_description: Playbook-driven automation for triaging alerts, executing responses, and orchestrating security workflows where agents are either operators or subjects. Distinct from Control Planes (policy enforcement) and Sandboxing (isolation) — this slot is about scripted, repeatable security actions. Example: Tracecat.

nodes.label: Control Planes
nodes.short_description: Centralized policy and governance control planes that enforce rules across fleets of agents and tools.
nodes.long_description: Administrative layers that set, distribute, and enforce security policy across many agents — access control, audit, kill-switches. Sits above individual guardrail libraries, providing org-level governance. Example: Cordum.

nodes.label: Red Team
nodes.short_description: Offensive security tooling for probing agents and LLMs for vulnerabilities, jailbreaks, and unsafe behaviors.
nodes.long_description: Red-team frameworks that attack agent systems to surface weaknesses before adversaries do — fuzzing prompts, chaining exploits, simulating malicious tool use. Distinct from evals (which measure capability) in that the goal is adversarial discovery.

nodes.label: Prompt Injection Scanners
nodes.short_description: Scanners and test harnesses specifically targeting prompt injection and LLM jailbreak vulnerabilities.
nodes.long_description: Tools that automate discovery of prompt-injection weaknesses in LLM applications via curated attack libraries and fuzzing (Garak, PyRIT, Promptfoo Redteam). A narrow slot under Red Team focused on the input-manipulation attack class, not broader adversarial ML.

nodes.label: Sandboxing
nodes.short_description: Isolated execution environments where agents can run code, commands, or tools without risking the host.
nodes.long_description: Lightweight VMs, microVMs, or container-based sandboxes purpose-built for agent code execution and tool invocation. Distinct from general dev sandboxes — optimized for short-lived, untrusted LLM-generated workloads. Example: Microsandbox.

nodes.label: Tools & Integrations
nodes.short_description: Tools, actions, and integration layers that connect agents to the outside world — MCP, standards, and action surfaces.
nodes.long_description: The top-level domain for everything that extends an agent's reach: tool protocols (MCP), standards bodies, and the concrete action surfaces agents invoke (browsers, APIs, wallets, voice). Sits between frameworks/runtimes and the real-world systems agents manipulate.

nodes.label: Actions
nodes.short_description: Concrete action surfaces agents invoke — browsers, computer use, APIs, wallets, voice, and third-party integrations.
nodes.long_description: The "hands" of the agent: the actual effectors that let it browse, click, pay, extract, or call services. Distinct from MCP (the protocol) and Standards (the specs) — this slot is where real-world capabilities live, grouped by modality and target system.

nodes.label: AI Browsers
nodes.short_description: Full browser products with native AI/agent capabilities built into the user-facing application.
nodes.long_description: End-user browsers (Chromium forks or new builds) where agentic features are first-class — not scriptable automation stacks but actual browsers a human or agent drives. Distinct from Browser Automation (programmatic control) and Browser Tooling (libraries). Example: BrowserOS.

nodes.label: Headless
nodes.short_description: Headless browsers designed for AI agents — no UI, optimized for programmatic navigation and scraping.
nodes.long_description: Browser engines built or tuned for agent workloads, exposing DOM/automation APIs without a rendered UI. In scope: Lightpanda, Steel Browser and similar headless runtimes. Out of scope: cloud browser infrastructure services and human-facing AI browsers.

nodes.label: Authenticated Tool Proxies
nodes.short_description: Hosted proxies that handle OAuth, credentials, and tool invocation on behalf of agents across many SaaS APIs.
nodes.long_description: Services that abstract auth and API surfaces so agents can call Gmail, Slack, Notion, etc. without managing tokens or per-API SDKs. Distinct from Integrations (generic connector libs) — the differentiator is managed auth and a unified tool surface. Examples: Arcade, Pica, Nango.

nodes.label: Browser Automation
nodes.short_description: Programmatic browser control stacks aimed at agent-driven automation and scraping workflows.
nodes.long_description: Automation frameworks and runtimes that drive real browsers under agent control — built on or beside Playwright/CDP. Distinct from AI Browsers (user-facing products) and Browser Tooling (helper libs layered on top). Includes cloud browser infrastructure as a child.

nodes.label: Cloud Browser Infrastructure
nodes.short_description: Hosted browser infrastructure providing managed sessions, proxies, and captchas for agent automation at scale.
nodes.long_description: Cloud services running fleets of real browsers that agents drive via Playwright/CDP, handling scaling, auth, and anti-bot concerns. In scope: Browserbase, Hyperbrowser, Anchor Browser. Out of scope: local headless browser engines and human-facing AI browsers.

nodes.label: Browser Tooling
nodes.short_description: Libraries and helpers layered on browser automation stacks to make them agent-friendly (DOM extraction, action planning).
nodes.long_description: Thin layers above Playwright/CDP that give agents structured affordances — accessibility snapshots, element selection, observation primitives. Distinct from full Browser Automation runtimes; these are the glue libraries agents use. Examples: Stagehand, Agent Browser.

nodes.label: Computer Use
nodes.short_description: Frameworks letting agents control a full desktop OS via keyboard, mouse, and screen — beyond just the browser.
nodes.long_description: OS-level agent control: platform-specific drivers, CLI-first approaches, and vision-based UI models. Distinct from Browser Automation in that the surface is the entire desktop/VM, not a web page. Example: cua.

nodes.label: CLI
nodes.short_description: Computer-use agents that drive the machine via CLI/shell rather than GUI interactions.
nodes.long_description: Runtimes that let agents operate a computer primarily through terminal commands and shell sessions. In scope: CLI-first computer-use harnesses. Out of scope: vision/GUI computer use and OS-specific GUI controllers.

nodes.label: Platform Specific
nodes.short_description: Computer-use tooling scoped to a specific operating system, leveraging native APIs for control.
nodes.long_description: OS-bound computer-use implementations that rely on platform accessibility, scripting, or automation APIs. In scope: macOS-, Windows-, or Linux-specific controllers. Out of scope: cross-platform vision-UI or CLI-based computer use.

nodes.label: macOS
nodes.short_description: macOS-native computer-use runtimes controlling the Mac via AppleScript, accessibility, or native APIs.
nodes.long_description: Agent harnesses that drive macOS specifically, using platform automation to control apps and windows. In scope: macOS-use, cmux and similar. Out of scope: cross-platform vision-based computer use and CLI-only harnesses.

nodes.label: Vision UI
nodes.short_description: Vision-based computer-use agents that interact with GUIs through screenshots and pixel-level grounding.
nodes.long_description: Systems using multimodal models to see the screen and emit clicks/keystrokes without DOM or accessibility access. In scope: vision-grounded GUI agents like UI-Venus. Out of scope: CLI-based and platform-native accessibility-driven computer use.

nodes.label: Data Extraction and Structuring
nodes.short_description: Services that turn web pages and unstructured sources into clean, structured data for agents.
nodes.long_description: Crawl-and-extract APIs that convert HTML, PDFs, and messy pages into markdown or JSON for LLM consumption. Typically a hosted API (Firecrawl) rather than a library. Distinct from Browser Automation — the goal is data output, not interaction.

nodes.label: Financial Tools
nodes.short_description: Toolkits giving agents financial capabilities — wallets, payment rails, and transaction execution.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and SDKs that let agents hold funds, sign transactions, and move money (crypto or fiat). Split into agent payment rails and agent wallets. Examples: GOAT, Coinbase AgentKit. Distinct from Identity protocols like AP2, which define the handshake rather than the execution.

nodes.label: Agent Payment Rails
nodes.short_description: Payment rails and protocols that let agents pay for services, APIs, or other agents programmatically.
nodes.long_description: Machine-payable infrastructure — settlement protocols, metering, and HTTP-native payment specs for agents. In scope: x402, Skyfire, Nevermined. Out of scope: agent wallets that hold funds and traditional payment APIs not agent-oriented.

nodes.label: Agent Wallets
nodes.short_description: Wallet infrastructure giving agents custody, signing, and balance management for on-chain or fiat funds.
nodes.long_description: Server-side and embedded wallets provisioned per agent to hold keys, sign transactions, and manage balances. In scope: Coinbase CDP AgentKit, Crossmint, Privy server wallets. Out of scope: payment rails/settlement protocols and general consumer wallets.

nodes.label: Integrations
nodes.short_description: Generic integration/connector libraries exposing many third-party APIs as agent-callable tools.
nodes.long_description: SDKs that bundle hundreds of SaaS API connectors into one tool surface for agents (Composio). Distinct from Authenticated Tool Proxies in that the focus is the connector catalog and SDK shape, not managed auth-as-a-service.

nodes.label: Marketplaces
nodes.short_description: Marketplaces where agents and their actions are listed, discovered, and transacted by other agents or humans.
nodes.long_description: Directories and storefronts for publishing or hiring agents and packaged actions. In scope: agent listing sites, discovery portals, commerce layers for agent services. Out of scope: MCP registries (see MCP > Registries & Marketplaces) and tool integration catalogs.

nodes.label: Software Extensions
nodes.short_description: Extensions that embed agentic actions into existing host software such as IDEs, notebooks, or editors.
nodes.long_description: Plugins and add-ons that expose agent capabilities inside a host application rather than standing alone. In scope: notebook assistants, IDE extensions, editor plugins delivering tool-call actions. Out of scope: standalone agents and browser/computer-use runtimes.

nodes.label: Upserting Tools
nodes.short_description: Tools that let agents insert or update records in external systems (CRMs, databases, SaaS) as a write-side action.
nodes.long_description: The write counterpart to extraction: action tools specialized in upserting structured data back into systems of record. In scope: CRM/DB upsert connectors and writeback utilities exposed to agents. Out of scope: read-only data extraction and general integration catalogs.

nodes.label: Voice
nodes.short_description: Voice action tools letting agents place calls, speak, and handle telephony-grade audio interactions.
nodes.long_description: Libraries and services that give agents voice as an action surface — outbound/inbound calls, IVR, real-time audio. In scope: voice agent SDKs and telephony runtimes used as tools. Out of scope: voice-first agent frameworks (Frameworks > Voice) and raw TTS/STT models.

nodes.label: Web Search APIs for Agents
nodes.short_description: Web search APIs purpose-built as agent tool calls, returning LLM-friendly results rather than SERP HTML.
nodes.long_description: Search-as-a-service endpoints designed for agent consumption: clean snippets, ranked passages, and retrieval-oriented responses. In scope: Tavily, Exa, Linkup, Serper and similar. Out of scope: general scraping, browser automation, and vector retrieval over private corpora.

nodes.label: MCP
nodes.short_description: Model Context Protocol ecosystem — servers, gateways, aggregators, registries, and third-party MCP implementations.
nodes.long_description: Everything in and around MCP, the emerging standard for exposing tools and context to LLMs. Covers server implementations, gateways that multiplex or proxy MCP traffic, aggregation layers, and marketplaces. Distinct from generic tool libraries — these are specifically MCP-shaped.

nodes.label: Aggregation
nodes.short_description: Services that aggregate many MCP servers behind a single unified interface for an agent client.
nodes.long_description: Aggregators fan out a client's MCP connection across multiple backend servers, multiplexing tools into one session. In scope: multi-server MCP hosts and bundlers. Out of scope: protocol gateways that translate transports, and registries that only list servers.

nodes.label: Gateways
nodes.short_description: MCP gateways that proxy, translate, or policy-enforce between MCP clients and backend servers.
nodes.long_description: Middleware sitting on the wire between MCP client and server — handling transport translation, auth, routing, observability, and policy. In scope: MCP proxies and context-forge style gateways. Out of scope: pure aggregators and server registries.

nodes.label: Registries & Marketplaces
nodes.short_description: Registries and marketplaces for discovering, publishing, and installing MCP servers.
nodes.long_description: Catalogs of MCP servers with metadata, install flows, and sometimes hosted execution. In scope: Smithery, PulseMCP, the official MCP Registry. Out of scope: gateways and aggregators that run servers, and non-MCP agent/action marketplaces.

nodes.label: Third Party
nodes.short_description: Third-party MCP servers wrapping external products or APIs that the vendor itself does not ship.
nodes.long_description: Community-built MCP servers exposing SaaS products, websites, or APIs as tools when no first-party server exists. In scope: independently maintained server implementations for specific services. Out of scope: official/vendor MCP servers and registries listing them.

nodes.label: Standards
nodes.short_description: Open standards and specifications for agent interoperability — ratified, proposed, and ecosystem-tied protocols.
nodes.long_description: Specification-level artifacts defining how agents, tools, and services interoperate. Segmented by maturity: ratified standards, proposals under discussion, and emerging standards tightly coupled to a single vendor's ecosystem. Distinct from MCP, which has its own dedicated branch.

nodes.label: Emerging (Ecosystem-Tied)
nodes.short_description: Emerging agent standards implemented inside a specific ecosystem or product rather than a neutral body.
nodes.long_description: Specs gaining traction through a particular platform or browser vendor before any cross-industry ratification. In scope: WebMCP and similar ecosystem-anchored proposals. Out of scope: fully ratified cross-vendor protocols and early paper-only proposals.

nodes.label: Proposed
nodes.short_description: Proposed agent-ecosystem standards still in draft — not yet widely implemented or ratified.
nodes.long_description: Early-stage specifications circulated for feedback: protocols, certifications, or interoperability drafts without committed adopters yet. In scope: draft specs like GitAgent or AIUC-1. Out of scope: ratified protocols and specs already shipping inside a specific ecosystem.

nodes.label: Ratified
nodes.short_description: Ratified, broadly adopted agent interoperability protocols backed by multi-vendor implementations.
nodes.long_description: Published specs with stable versions and shipping implementations across the ecosystem. In scope: MCP, A2A, AG-UI and similar. Out of scope: draft proposals and ecosystem-tied emerging specs.
